label: short
chain_of_thought:
Okay, let's see. I need to find the concentration of salt when I mix two different solutions. Hmm, the first solution is 3 liters of 20% salt. So the amount of salt in that would be 20% of 3 liters. Let me calculate that. 20% is 0.2 in decimal, right? So 0.2 times 3 is... 0.6 liters of salt.

Then the second solution is 7 liters of 10% salt. So that's 10% of 7 liters. 10% is 0.1, so 0.1 times 7 is 0.7 liters of salt. 

Now, when I mix them together, the total amount of salt will be 0.6 plus 0.7. Let me add those: 0.6 + 0.7 equals 1.3 liters of salt total.

The total volume of the mixture is 3 liters plus 7 liters, which is 10 liters. 

So the concentration is the total salt divided by the total volume. That's 1.3 liters divided by 10 liters. Let me do that division. 1.3 divided by 10 is 0.13. To convert that to a percentage, I multiply by 100. 0.13 times 100 is 13%. 

Wait, let me double-check my math. 20% of 3 is indeed 0.6. 10% of 7 is 0.7. Together, that's 1.3. Total volume is 10 liters. 1.3 divided by 10 is 0.13, so 13%. Yeah, that seems right. I don't think I made a mistake here. 

Alternatively, maybe I can think of it as weighted averages. The concentration would be (3*20% + 7*10%)/(3+7). Which is (60% + 70%)/10 = 130%/10 = 13%. Yep, same answer. Okay, so 13% must be correct.
